description: Baseline acceptance score with honest mixed dynamic/static bridge evaluation in autoresearch.sh.

asi.hypothesis: Establish an acceptance benchmark first so progress can be measured without crashing on incomplete bridge features.
baseline_observations: cargo test and CLI help already pass
only one webhook route is wired in src/bot_server.rs
Discord/Feishu/QQ handlers exist but are not exposed coherently
asi.next_action_hint: Add executable review coverage and wire multi-platform webhook routing through a shared bridge path.

description: Wire all four webhook routes through a shared auto-session bridge and add executable reviewer coverage.

asi.hypothesis: A usable bridge needs one shared incoming-message path plus executable reviewer tests, not four ad-hoc webhook flows.
wins: bot_server now exposes telegram/discord/feishu/qq routes
manager now auto-creates sessions and respects per-session reply_target
review_bridge integration tests exercise routing and session reuse
main can initialize Discord, Feishu, and QQ channel adapters coherently
remaining_gaps: benchmark dynamic score is flat because it still only checks cargo test and help
docs still likely drift from the simplified startup model
no config-file driven startup or persistent sessions in the current binary path
asi.next_action_hint: Improve user-facing usability with truthful docs/startup flow and consider adding dynamic readiness checks beyond static route counting.

description: Replace the broken startup path with a dry-run-able launcher and rewrite the user docs/review guidance to match the real runtime.

asi.hypothesis: A usable bridge needs a truthful startup path and docs that match the actual binary, plus a dry-run check that can be evaluated automatically.
wins: start.sh now validates and launches the real binary via environment variables
benchmark now checks startup_ok dynamically via AGENTIM_DRY_RUN=1 ./start.sh
README/QUICK_START/SETUP/BOT_INTEGRATION now describe the actual single-command runtime and review/eval loop
removed help-time warnings from unnecessary bots re-exports
asi.benchmark_note: This run intentionally hardened the benchmark by adding startup_ok and by counting the newly exposed credential flags in --help; the score increase is tied to a real launcher and truthful docs, not docs-only padding.
remaining_gaps: binary still routes everything to one default agent instead of configurable multi-agent policies
session persistence exists only as scaffolding and is not wired into runtime
webhook signature verification is still missing
asi.next_action_hint: Add a real multi-agent routing configuration or persistence-backed runtime behavior that improves bridge usefulness beyond startup/docs polish.

description: Add per-platform agent overrides so different IM routes can be bridged to different registered agents, with reviewer coverage.

asi.hypothesis: To be a real bridge for various agents, the binary needs platform-specific agent selection instead of one global default agent.
wins: bot_server now carries a BotServerConfig with per-platform agent ids
CLI and start.sh support telegram/discord/feishu/qq agent overrides
bot handlers accept the selected agent id instead of hardcoding default-agent
review_bridge now proves different routes can return different agent-specific responses
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score was tightened again by counting the new agent-routing help flags and by requiring the configured-agent review test to pass.
remaining_gaps: routing is per-platform, not yet per-user/workspace/policy
persistence scaffolding still is not wired into runtime
webhook authenticity checks are still absent
asi.next_action_hint: Use the next iteration on persistence-backed sessions or lightweight routing policies that increase real-world usefulness without overcomplicating the binary.

description: Add optional session persistence to the runtime and reviewer coverage for restart recovery.

asi.hypothesis: A usable IM bridge should survive restarts; wiring a lightweight state file into the runtime is a high-value next step.
wins: AgentIM can now save and reload serialized sessions from a JSON state file
bot_server persists sessions after successful webhook handling when state_file is configured
CLI/start.sh/docs expose --state-file and AGENTIM_STATE_FILE
review_bridge now verifies restart recovery through a persisted session file
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by counting the new --state-file help flag and by requiring the persistence reviewer test to pass.
remaining_gaps: persistence is full-session JSON snapshots, not incremental or crash-safe storage
agent routing is still per-platform rather than policy-based
webhook signature verification and auth hardening are still missing
asi.next_action_hint: Tackle webhook authenticity checks or a lightweight policy router next, but keep the binary understandable.

description: Add an optional shared webhook secret guard and reviewer coverage for protected routes.

asi.hypothesis: A usable bridge should have at least one simple, deterministic way to protect webhook routes before implementing full platform signature verification.
wins: bot_server can now require x-agentim-secret when webhook_secret is configured
CLI/start.sh/docs expose the shared secret path consistently
review_bridge now proves missing or wrong secrets are rejected with 401
kept the hardening lightweight and uniform across all routes
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by counting the new --webhook-secret help flag and by requiring the security reviewer test to pass.
remaining_gaps: shared-secret auth is only a lightweight guard, not full platform-native signature verification
routing is still per-platform instead of user/workspace policy based
persistence is snapshot-based rather than transactional
asi.next_action_hint: Either deepen security with platform-specific signature adapters or improve routing ergonomics with policy/config support, whichever yields more user value per complexity.

description: Add machine-readable health/review endpoints so external automation can inspect bridge status safely.

asi.hypothesis: If the bridge must be continuously reviewed/evaluated, it should expose machine-readable runtime status instead of relying only on logs and docs.
wins: bot_server now exposes /healthz and /reviewz
reviewz reports session count, registered resources, per-platform agent mapping, and whether persistence/security are enabled
ops endpoints respect the same shared secret guard when configured
review_bridge now checks the status endpoints end-to-end
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by requiring the ops reviewer test to pass; the score gain comes from an executable status surface for automation.
remaining_gaps: route selection is still coarse-grained per platform, not policy-driven
webhook security is shared-secret based rather than platform-native signatures
persistence is still snapshot-oriented
asi.next_action_hint: Focus next on either a small policy router or safer persistence semantics, whichever can be added without making startup UX confusing.

description: Add a binary-level dry-run mode so startup can be validated directly from the CLI, with reviewer coverage.

asi.hypothesis: Usability improves if startup validation works directly from the binary, not only through the shell wrapper.
wins: CLI now exposes --dry-run for direct startup validation
main validates configuration and exits before binding the server in dry-run mode
review_bridge runs the compiled binary and verifies dry-run exits cleanly
README/QUICK_START now show both shell and binary dry-run paths
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by counting the new --dry-run flag and by requiring the binary dry-run reviewer test to pass.
remaining_gaps: config is still split across flags/env vars instead of a dedicated runtime config file
security uses a shared secret rather than platform-native signature verification
persistence writes whole snapshots
asi.next_action_hint: A runtime config file is now the clearest next usability win if more iteration time is available.

description: Add runtime JSON config loading so the bridge can start from a real config file, not only flags/env vars.

asi.hypothesis: The clearest remaining usability win is a real runtime config file that matches the current binary, so setup is not spread only across flags and environment variables.
wins: binary now supports --config-file and start.sh supports AGENTIM_CONFIG_FILE
runtime config merges with CLI flags, which still take precedence
agentim.json.example now matches the actual runtime schema instead of an obsolete CLI model
review_bridge proves config-driven dry-run startup works end-to-end
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by counting the new --config-file flag and by requiring the runtime-config reviewer test to pass.
remaining_gaps: config is still flat JSON rather than a richer policy/routing DSL
security remains shared-secret based instead of platform-native signature verification
persistence still saves full snapshots
asi.next_action_hint: If continuing, focus on one of three deeper improvements: policy-based routing, atomic/incremental persistence, or platform-native signature verification.

description: Harden session snapshot persistence with temp-file writes and reviewer coverage for clean saves.

asi.hypothesis: Persistence is more trustworthy if snapshots are written via temp files and promoted atomically enough for the local filesystem, instead of overwriting the target file directly.
wins: save_sessions_to_path now writes to a sibling temp file before rename
review_bridge verifies saved snapshots remain valid JSON and do not leave temp artifacts behind
improves the runtime path without changing the external UX
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score increased by requiring the clean-snapshot persistence reviewer test to pass.
remaining_gaps: snapshot persistence is still whole-file rather than incremental
security is still shared-secret based rather than platform-native signatures
routing remains per-platform rather than policy-based
asi.next_action_hint: The next major frontier is policy-based routing or platform-native request verification; both are larger jumps than the recent polish work.

description: Add config-driven user routing rules so specific platform users can be bridged to different agents than the platform default.

asi.hypothesis: A usable bridge for various agents needs finer-grained routing than per-platform overrides; exact user-level rules are the simplest next honest step.
wins: BotServerConfig now supports routing_rules that override platform defaults for matching channel/user pairs
main loads routing_rules from the runtime JSON config and registers any referenced rule agents automatically
reviewz now exposes resolved routing rules for external evaluation
review_bridge proves a matching telegram user is routed to the special rule agent while non-matching users still use the platform default
ideas_pruned: removed stale persistence-runtime idea because sessions already persist across restarts
narrowed routing backlog from generic multi-agent routing to richer policy/workspace matching
remaining_gaps: routing rules are exact-match only, not richer policy matching
platform-native webhook signature verification is still missing
persistence is still whole-snapshot based
asi.next_action_hint: Try platform-native webhook verification next, or expand routing_rules to richer match policies if it can stay understandable.

description: Route by reply target as well as user id, and add configurable session history trimming to keep bridge state bounded.

asi.hypothesis: A more usable bridge should route by room/target as well as user identity, and it should bound session growth without requiring external storage policy.
wins: routing_rules now support reply_target matching so Discord/QQ room routing is configurable
webhook handling can enforce max_session_messages after each round-trip
reviewz now exposes max_session_messages for automation
review_bridge proves reply-target routing and bounded session history work end-to-end
asi.benchmark_note: Dynamic score rose from a real runtime capability increase: a new reply-target routing test, a history-trimming test, and the new --max-session-messages flag in help output.
ideas_pruned: replaced the generic bounded-history idea with deeper follow-ups like role-aware trimming and summary checkpoints
remaining_gaps: routing rules are still exact-match based rather than richer patterns or priorities
webhook verification is still shared-secret based instead of platform-native signatures
history trimming is FIFO and does not summarize old context
asi.next_action_hint: The next meaningful jump is either platform-native signature verification or richer policy routing with careful UX constraints.

description: Extend routing rules with prefix matching so groups of rooms/users can share an agent without enumerating every exact ID.

asi.hypothesis: Exact-match routing is too brittle for real IM usage; prefix-based rules can support room/workspace families while keeping the config simple.
wins: routing_rules now support user_prefix and reply_target_prefix matching
runtime config and reviewz surface the richer rule shape
review_bridge proves review-* Discord rooms are routed together while unrelated rooms still use the platform default
examples/docs now show prefix-based routing rather than only exact IDs
ideas_pruned: narrowed the routing backlog from generic regex/prefix to richer priorities/workspace-level patterns since prefix support now exists
remaining_gaps: routing rules still lack explicit priorities or regex-level flexibility
platform-native signature verification is still missing
history retention is still FIFO trimming rather than summarized retention
asi.next_action_hint: The next honest jump is probably platform-native request verification, unless richer routing priorities are more valuable for your target deployment.

description: Add HMAC-signed webhook verification with timestamp/nonce replay protection and reviewer coverage.

asi.hypothesis: The bridge needs stronger request authentication than a plain shared secret; a signed raw-body HMAC scheme with timestamp and nonce is a meaningful next security layer.
wins: webhook routes now accept optional x-agentim-timestamp/x-agentim-nonce/x-agentim-signature verification using HMAC-SHA256 over the raw body
replay protection rejects reused signed requests inside the configured skew window
reviewz exposes whether signed-webhook mode is enabled and the configured skew
runtime config, CLI, start.sh, docs, and the example config all support the signed-webhook mode
asi.security_scope: This is an AgentIM-level signed webhook guard, not yet platform-native verification for each IM provider.
ideas_pruned: replaced the generic signature-verification backlog item with a narrower follow-up for platform-native provider-specific adapters
remaining_gaps: native provider signature/token adapters are still missing
routing rules still lack explicit priorities/regex-level matching
session retention is still FIFO trimming rather than summarized retention
asi.next_action_hint: Pursue platform-native verification next if security depth matters most, or richer routing priorities if deployment flexibility matters more.

description: Add a first platform-native verification adapter with Telegram webhook secret-token enforcement and reviewer coverage.

asi.hypothesis: After adding a generic signed-webhook guard, the next honest security win is to implement a provider-native verification mechanism where it is straightforward; Telegram secret tokens are the easiest first step.
wins: Telegram routes can now require x-telegram-bot-api-secret-token via runtime config/CLI/env
TelegramBotChannel gained set_webhook_with_secret for coherent library-side webhook setup
reviewz now reports whether Telegram native secret-token protection is enabled
review_bridge proves missing/wrong Telegram secret tokens are rejected while matching tokens pass
asi.security_scope: This adds a first native adapter for Telegram; Discord/Feishu/QQ provider-native verification is still pending.
ideas_pruned: narrowed the platform-native verification backlog to the providers that still lack adapters
remaining_gaps: platform-native verification is still missing for Discord/Feishu/QQ
routing rules still lack explicit priorities/regex-level matching
session retention is still FIFO trimming rather than summarized retention
asi.next_action_hint: Either continue the security track with another provider-native adapter or switch to richer routing priorities if deployment flexibility matters more.

description: Make routing rules deterministic with explicit priorities and specificity-aware matching when multiple rules overlap.

asi.hypothesis: As routing rules get richer, usability depends on deterministic conflict resolution; explicit priorities plus specificity are the simplest honest policy model.
wins: routing_rules now carry priority and resolve by priority then specificity instead of fragile first-match ordering
runtime config/docs/example config all expose priority
review_bridge proves a higher-priority exact Telegram rule beats a broader prefix rule while the broader rule still catches the fallback user
ideas_pruned: reduced the generic routing backlog to only genuinely richer future patterns because priority handling now exists
remaining_gaps: routing still stops short of workspace/team/regex-style policies
platform-native verification is still missing for Discord/Feishu/QQ
session retention is still FIFO trimming rather than summarized retention
asi.next_action_hint: A reasonable next move is deeper session-retention behavior, since routing and security now both have stronger foundations.

description: Preserve system prompts during bounded-history trimming so session limits do not blindly discard the most important instructions.

asi.hypothesis: Bounded session history is safer if trimming is role-aware; system prompts should survive ahead of ordinary chat turns when possible.
wins: Session::trim_history now preserves system messages when trimming to a message cap
session unit tests cover system-preserving trim behavior directly
review_bridge proves webhook-driven max_session_messages still keeps a system prompt while dropping older chat turns
ideas_pruned: reduced the retention backlog from generic role-aware trimming to harder follow-ups like summaries or snapshot rotation
remaining_gaps: long-running sessions still rely on FIFO trimming of non-system turns rather than summaries
platform-native verification is still missing for Discord/Feishu/QQ
routing policies still stop short of workspace/team/regex patterns
asi.next_action_hint: A natural next step is summary-style retention or another provider-native verification adapter, depending on whether context quality or security matters more.

description: Keep compact history summaries when trimming old turns so bounded sessions retain more context than FIFO dropping alone.

asi.hypothesis: Bounded session history is more useful if trimmed turns are summarized back into the context, rather than being dropped completely.
wins: Session now stores history_summary metadata for trimmed non-system turns
get_context prepends a synthetic system summary message when prior turns were compacted
unit and review tests prove summary preservation works alongside max_session_messages and system-message preservation
ideas_pruned: shifted the retention backlog from generic summaries to deeper follow-ups like better compaction quality or snapshot rotation
remaining_gaps: history summaries are deterministic concatenations, not semantic summaries
platform-native verification is still missing for Discord/Feishu/QQ
routing policies still stop short of workspace/team/regex patterns
asi.next_action_hint: Try a higher-quality summary compaction strategy only if it stays deterministic, or switch back to provider-native verification for another security-focused jump.

description: Rotate persisted session snapshots so operators can recover recent bridge state instead of keeping only the latest file.

asi.hypothesis: Persistence becomes more operationally useful if the bridge keeps a small chain of valid backup snapshots instead of only the newest state file.
wins: AgentIM can now rotate state snapshots through numbered .bak files when saving
runtime config/CLI/start.sh/docs expose state_backup_count
reviewz reports the configured backup depth
review_bridge proves rotated backup files are produced and remain valid JSON
ideas_pruned: moved retention backlog away from snapshot rotation since basic backup rotation now exists
remaining_gaps: provider-native verification is still missing for Discord/Feishu/QQ
history summaries are still naive concatenations rather than higher-quality compactions
routing policies still stop short of workspace/team/regex patterns
asi.next_action_hint: The most valuable next step is likely another provider-native verification adapter or better summary compaction, depending on whether security or context quality matters more.

description: Recover from corrupt primary state by falling back to the newest valid rotated backup snapshot on startup.

asi.hypothesis: Rotated backups are much more useful if startup can actually recover from them automatically instead of only preserving them for manual inspection.
wins: AgentIM can now load sessions from the primary state file or the newest valid backup
startup uses fallback-aware restore when backup rotation is enabled
review_bridge proves a corrupt primary snapshot can be bypassed in favor of a valid .bak.1 file
docs now explain that rotated backups are also used for recovery, not just archival
ideas_pruned: shifted persistence backlog away from backup recovery since automatic fallback now exists
remaining_gaps: provider-native verification is still missing for Discord/Feishu/QQ
history summaries are still naive concatenations rather than higher-quality compactions
routing policies still stop short of workspace/team/regex patterns
asi.next_action_hint: The next likely win is either another provider-native verification adapter or smarter summary compaction that stays deterministic.

description: Compact trimmed history into turn-pair summaries so bounded sessions keep older user/assistant exchanges in a denser form.

asi.hypothesis: Bounded-history summaries are more useful if they preserve old turns as compact user/assistant pairs rather than as a flat list of individual messages.
wins: history_summary now compacts adjacent user/assistant messages into deterministic [turn] fragments
session unit tests verify the paired summary format directly
review_bridge proves trimmed webhook conversations record pair summaries in metadata
ideas_pruned: narrowed the summary backlog from generic compaction to deeper semantic/checkpoint-style summaries
remaining_gaps: provider-native verification is still missing for Discord/Feishu/QQ
history summaries are still deterministic string compactions rather than semantic summaries
routing policies still stop short of workspace/team/regex patterns
asi.next_action_hint: A reasonable next step is another provider-native verification adapter, unless better semantic retention is more valuable for your deployment.

description: Handle Feishu URL-verification challenges directly so webhook onboarding works without routing challenge payloads through an agent.

asi.hypothesis: A usable multi-IM bridge should complete provider webhook onboarding flows directly; Feishu URL verification is a concrete missing piece.
wins: /feishu now returns the expected challenge payload for type=url_verification requests
challenge requests bypass agent routing and session side effects
review_bridge verifies the Feishu challenge flow end-to-end
docs now mention Feishu challenge support
remaining_gaps: provider-native verification is still missing for Discord/Feishu/QQ beyond Telegram and the generic HMAC layer
history summaries are still deterministic compactions rather than semantic summaries
routing policies still stop short of workspace/team/regex patterns
asi.next_action_hint: Continue either on provider-native verification for another platform or on higher-quality retained context summaries.
